content:
{
  "title": "Execution-Guided Chain-of-Thought Prompting for Code Generation",
  "problem": "Current code generation models often fail to produce syntactically correct or semantically valid code, especially for complex tasks that require multi-step reasoning. Prompting strategies to elicit better step-wise reasoning are underexplored in the coding domain.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM code generation is critical for developer productivity and automation, but standard prompting methods yield brittle or non-functional code for many non-trivial problems. As models grow in size and capability, leveraging more structured reasoning via prompting may unlock further gains. Execution feedback, rarely used at inference-time in prompting, is now computationally feasible and could help models self-correct.",
  "method": "We propose Execution-Guided Chain-of-Thought (EG-CoT) prompting: prompts instruct the model to produce code iteratively in step-by-step reasoning blocks, and after each block, the partial code is executed in a sandbox. If an execution error occurs, the model is reprompted to reflect on the error and revise the last step before continuing. This interleaves code output, execution feedback, and error-aware reflection within a single prompt, realized via in-context exemplars that demonstrate this process. The method is model-agnostic and requires no model fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate EG-CoT on the HumanEval and MBPP datasets, comparing against vanilla prompting, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and Self-Consistency decoding. Metrics include pass@1, pass@10, and syntactic correctness rates. A core experiment will ablate the execution-feedback loop to isolate its contribution."
}
